items.q: What is the difference between fixed and variable costs?
items.correct: Fixed costs do not change with output level (e.g. rent, salaries); variable costs change in proportion to output (e.g. raw materials, hourly wages).
items.misconception: Fixed costs are paid every month and variable costs are paid weekly.
items.random_wrong: Fixed costs are the cheapest costs and variable costs are the most expensive.
items.why: The distinction is about behaviour with output, not payment frequency. Rent is fixed because producing more units doesn't increase it; flour for a bakery is variable because more loaves means more flour.

items.q: What three Ps are added to make the extended marketing mix (7Ps)?
items.correct: People (staff/service quality), Process (how the service is delivered), and Physical Evidence (tangible cues — uniforms, packaging, branding) — added to make the 4Ps relevant to services marketing.
items.misconception: The 7Ps are just the 4Ps repeated with different words.
items.random_wrong: The 7Ps include Profit, Planning and Performance.
items.why: The extended mix specifically addresses services, where customer experience depends on staff, process and atmospherics — not just product. Students treat 4Ps and 7Ps as interchangeable, missing what services marketing requires.

items.q: How does HRM differ from traditional personnel management?
items.correct: HRM treats people as a strategic asset and integrates human resource policies with business strategy; personnel management is more administrative and reactive, handling hiring, pay and disputes.
items.misconception: HRM and personnel are exactly the same thing — just a name change.
items.random_wrong: HRM only applies to large multinational companies.
items.why: The shift from personnel to HRM is conceptual — from administrative function to strategic partner. Students treat the relabel as cosmetic, missing the strategic management literature behind it.

items.q: Porter's three generic strategies are cost leadership, differentiation and:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Focus. “Diversification” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The 'Blue Ocean' concept was proposed by:
items.why: Correct answer: Kim and Mauborgne. “Porter” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many generic strategies did Porter identify?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 strategies. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
